Browser paths failed: Chrome/Computer Use approval denied for com.google.Chrome; Playwright CLI could not install because npm registry lookup failed (ENOTFOUND registry.npmjs.org); one-off codex exec worker could not start because /Users/liamvan/.codex session/state DB access is read-only/permission denied. Required FRQ awaiting Liam approval before submit. FRQ 1: Describe your experience in the AI ecosystem - whether working at, with, or selling to AI companies. Draft: I have not been in a pure sales role selling to AI companies, but I have built and supported AI-adjacent products and developer workflows that mirror the technical urgency of AI-native teams. At Oracle, I built an LLM-powered recommendation experience for OCI learning content and later designed AI-assisted blackbox API testing for cross-cloud database services, including prompt design, auth/spec parsing, Robot Framework setup, and onboarding docs. In my own projects, I built Fantasy Wizard, an AI advisor that turns live Sleeper, ESPN, and Yahoo data into roster, draft, waiver, and trade guidance, and SplitCheck, which uses AI receipt parsing inside a mobile-first workflow. Across those products, I observed that AI teams care deeply about speed-to-value, reliable data pipelines, clear integration paths, and product surfaces that help technical users trust outputs quickly. FRQ 2: This role requires both technical depth (writing code snippets, designing integrations) and the ability to drive deals independently. Share an example that demonstrates both your technical hands-on skills and your ability to own customer relationships end-to-end. Draft: In Oracle's GCP integration work, I owned GA readiness for customer-facing OCI database services on GCP, validating BaseDB, ADBS, Exadata, backup, restore, Data Guard, and API workflows across console, CLI, and automation surfaces. When canary issues or release blockers appeared, I investigated logs and Grafana/T2 metrics, reproduced failures with GCloud/OCI scripts, coordinated fixes with Oracle and GCP engineers, and wrote reusable validation/onboarding notes so other engineers could repeat the workflow. The work required hands-on implementation depth, but also end-to-end ownership: identifying blockers early, communicating impact clearly, aligning cross-functional stakeholders, and keeping the integration moving toward a reliable customer launch. awaiting Liam approval |

Draft: I am excited by the chance to build AI-powered GTM automation that turns messy customer and operational workflows into reliable internal tools. Why would we be excited to work with you? Draft: You would get an engineer who is comfortable owning ambiguous problems end to end: I write production-minded backend and full-stack code, build tests and tooling around the systems I touch, and communicate clearly when debugging across teams. At Oracle I have owned cross-cloud readiness work, built reusable validation workflows, and helped turn unclear release blockers into reproducible issues and durable fixes. I would bring that same practical, reliable style to Aircall's GTM AI automation team. |
